rulings.Case Title: SOUTHERN BLEACHER COMPANY, INC et al VS. JOHN DUNCAN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Action For Delay In Prosecution; Memorandum In Support; Declaratin Of Evan Adams
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, December 19, 2018, Line 1, REAL PARTY IN INTEREST JULIE SU's Motion To Dismiss Action For Delay In Prosecution Real Party in Interest Julie Su's motion to dismiss action for delay in prosecution is granted. This case is now dismissed because it was not brought to trial or a hearing within five years of the date it was filed. Petitioners have not shown why the five year statute should not apply to this case nor have they provided any evidence supporting any statutory exception to the five year statute. The declaration of Robert Fried, the sole item of evidence submitted by petitioners, does not attach any documents. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Su is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: MARY NICHOLS VS. MARK STREHLOW ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Notice Of Motion To Compel Third Party Witness John Motgomery To Answer Questions And To Produce Documents Pursuant To Deposition Subpoena
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, December 19, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF MARY NICHOLS' Motion To Compel Third Party Witness John Motgomery To Answer Questions And To Produce Documents Pursuant To Deposition Subpoena Plaintiff Mary Nichols' motion to compel third party witness John Montgomery to answer questions and to produce documents pursuant to deposition subpoena is granted. Mr. Montgomery is required to attend a further session of his deposition not exceeding three hours and may not refuse to answer any questions on the grounds of attorney-client privilege or attorney work product regarding his communications with and work for defendant Mark Strehelow pertaining to the Waller Street property including all facts and opinions stated in his declaration filed in this case. Neither Mr. Montgomery nor Mr. Strehelow has sufficiently shown that any communications that Mr. Montgomery had with or work he performed on behalf of Mr. Strehelow were in Mr. Montgomery's capacity as an attorney. Mr. Montgomery must produce all responsive documents called for in the subpoena at least three business days before the next session of his deposition and may not withhold any documents on the grounds of either attorney-client privilege or attorney work product. Because the contention that Mr. Montgomery had communications with and performed work for Mr. Strehelow in his capacity as an attorney did not lack substantial justification, Ms. Nichols' request for monetary sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms.Nichols is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ASHLEY RUST VS. SENDHUB, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Ash Rust, Garrett Johnson, Ryan Pfeffer, And John Fallone To Compel Production Of Documents; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, December 19, 2018, Line 2, PLAINTIFF ASHLEY RUST, GARRETT JOHNSON, RYAN PFEFFER, JOHN FALLONE's Motion to Compel Production Of Documents. Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing Required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: PATRICIA KUBICHEK VS. KIMPTON SIR FRANCISCO DRAKE HOTEL A PRIVATE ENTITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, December 19, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANTs KIMPTON SIR FRANCIS DRAKE HOTEL, KIMPTON HOTEL & RESTAURANT GROUP, LLC, and KHRG EMPLOYER, LLC's Motion To Compel Arbitration. Defendants Kimpton Sir Francis Drake Hotel, Kimpton Hotel & Restaurant Group, LLC, and KHRG Employer, LLC's motion to compel arbitration is granted. Plaintiff Patricia Kubichek entered into an arbitration agreement with defendants and the claims Ms. Kubichek alleges in her complaint are "employment-related disputes" as that phrase is defined in the parties' arbitration agreement. Ms. Kubichek's arguments based on her limited proficiency in English lack merit. (Ramos v. Westlake Services LLC (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 674, 687 ("Under the general contract principles ¿ the fact that [plaintiff] signed a contract in a language he may not have completely understood would not bar enforcement of the arbitration agreement. If [plaintiff] did not speak or understand English sufficiently to comprehend the English Contract, he should have had it read or explained to him.")). The agreement's language that the parties agree to submit to binding arbitration any employment related disputes "which are not resolved though the Property's employee appeal procedure or another informal means" is fairly construed to provide that all unresolved employment related disputes must be submitted to arbitration. This construction is consistent with the provision that an employee is not required to go through preliminary steps of dispute resolution before submitting a dispute to arbitration and that the parties have waived their right to a jury trial. Even if Ms. Kubichek's interpretation was adopted and the agreement's language was construed to mean that as a condition precedent to the employee's duty to arbitrate the employer is obliged to engage in an informal dispute resolution, there is no evidence that Ms. Kubicheck timely sought to appeal her termination through the employee appeal procedure or to resolve her claims through other informal means or that her employer refused to engage in such dispute resolution mechanisms. Assuming without deciding that the arbitration agreement had a high degree of procedural unconscionability, the agreement is enforceable because Ms. Kubichek has failed to show that the agreement contains any substantive unconscionable provisions. (Pinnacle Museum Tower Assn. v. Pinnacle Market Development (US), LLC (2012) 55 Cal.4th 223, 247 ("Both procedural unconscionability and substantive unconscionability must be shown, but 'they need not be present in the same degree' and are evaluated on 'a sliding scale.'") Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: DARRELL RINERSON VS. DAVID DYER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, December 19, 2018, Line 6, DEFENDANT DAVID DYER's Motion To Strike Complaint Defendant David Dyer's anti-SLAPP motion to strike the entirety of the complaint filed by plaintiff Darrell Rinerson is granted as to the thirteenth and seventeenth causes of action for declaratory relief and conspiracy and denied as to all other causes of action. Mr. Dyer has not satisfied his first prong burden of showing that the claims arise from activity protected by the anti-SLAPP statute as to the first through eighth causes of action. Those claims arise from conduct allegedly committed by Mr. Dyer prior to his termination from ELI. While this lawsuit was proposed in emails in an effort to induce Mr. Dyer to settle his DLSE claims, the first through eighth causes of action are based on separate alleged wrongful behavior unrelated to Mr. Dyer's protected petitioning of the DLSE. "[A] claim may be struck only if the speech or petitioning activity itself is the wrong complained of¿" (Park v. Board of Trustees of California State University (2017) 2 Cal.5th 1057, 1060.) The ninth through twelfth and fourteenth through sixteenth causes of action are not alleged against Mr. Dyer and thus he lacks standing to seek strike those claims. Mr. Dyer satisfied his first prong burden as to the thirteenth and seventeenth causes of action and Mr. Rinerson failed to satisfy his second prong burden of showing that those claims have minimal merit. The thirteenth cause of action for declaratory relief seeks to negate the effect the DLSE award and declare that debts are owed to Mr. Rinerson and ELI. This claim arises from Mr. Dyer's protected activity of petitioning DLSE for relief from allegedly owed compensation that was unpaid. (Levy v. City of Santa Monica (2004) 114 Cal.App.4th 1252 (claim seeking declaratory relief regarding administrative agency findings arises from anti-SLAPP statute protected activity).) The seventeenth cause of action for conspiracy also arises from Mr. Dyer's petitioning activity with DLSE. Mr. Rinerson did not present sufficient evidence to show that either the thirteenth or seventeenth cause of action has the required minimal merit to defeat an anti-SLAPP motion at the second prong stage. Because Mr. Dyer prevailed on only two of seventeen claims on this motion and did not achieved a significant reduction in the scope of the case yet his motion was not frivolous, both sides' request for attorneys' fees are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Rinerson is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ERIC ALEXANDER VS. RACHEL WHETSTONE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Rachel Whetstone'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And For An Order Staying The Action. *** Redacted Version ***
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, December 19, 2018, Line 8, Defendant Rachel Whetstone'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And For An Order Staying The Action. *** Redacted Version *** Defendant Rachel Whetstone's motion to compel arbitration is granted and this case is stayed pending conclusion of the arbitration proceedings. Unlike Brodke v. Alphatec Spine Inc. (2008) 160 Cal.App.4th 1569, 1574, Ms. Whetstone has affirmatively asserted that she is a signatory to an existing valid written agreement containing an arbitration clause. Since plaintiff Eric Alexander's complaint seeks to enforce the terms of this written agreement as a third party beneficiary, Mr. Alexander is equitably estopped from repudiating the agreement's arbitration clause and from avoiding arbitration of his breach of contract claim that is within the scope of the arbitration clause. (JSM Tuscany, LLC v. Superior Court (2011) 193 Cal.App.4th 1222, 1239-1240 (when a "plaintiff is suing on a contract-on the basis that, even though the plaintiff was not a party to the contract, the plaintiff is nonetheless entitled to recover for its breach, the plaintiff should be equitably estopped from repudiating the contract's arbitration clause . . . Additionally, a nonsignatory can be compelled to arbitrate when it is suing as a third-party beneficiary of the contract containing the arbitration clause.")). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Whetstone is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)
